label: Planting
heading: Cane Stalks Set in Fertile Soil
body: Farmers cut healthy cane into sections called setts - each with 2–3 nodes that sprout into new plants. Laid in rows 1.5m apart in well-tilled earth, each sett holds the potential for a full season's harvest.
facts: Planted by hand in rows;Each sett grows a full stalk;Season begins with the rains

label: Growing
heading: Months of Sun and Soil
body: The cane rises up to 4 metres tall over 6–12 months, drinking in sunlight and rain. Farmers tend the crop naturally - no synthetic fertilisers, no pesticides. Just land, light, and patience.
facts: Grows up to 4 metres tall;6–12 month growing cycle;No synthetic fertilisers used

label: Harvesting
heading: Cut at the Peak of Sweetness
body: When sugar content peaks, stalks are cut close to the ground - by hand or machine. The root system stays intact. From these same roots, a ratoon crop will grow back next season without replanting.
facts: Cut at peak natural sugar;Roots left to regrow (ratoon);Harvest by hand or machine

label: Transport
heading: Farm to Press the Same Day
body: Freshly cut cane is bundled and moved to the pressing point within hours. Speed is everything - cane loses its sweetness quickly once cut. Same-day transport means you taste the field, not the journey.
facts: Transported within hours;Freshness locked in;Direct farm-to-press route

label: Pressing
heading: Cold Crushed by Steel Rollers
body: Stalks are fed through heavy steel rollers that extract every drop of juice through pure mechanical pressure. No heat, no chemicals - just force. The dry fibre that remains is called bagasse, and nothing goes to waste.
facts: 100% mechanical extraction;No heat or chemicals used;Bagasse separated for reuse

label: Zero Waste
heading: Bagasse - The Fibre Lives On
body: The dry pulp left after pressing is not discarded. It is burned as biomass to power the press, formed into eco paper and packaging, or composted back into the fields - returning nutrients to the soil that grew the cane.
facts: Powers the press as biomass;Made into eco paper;Composted back to soil

label: Full Circle
heading: Roots Regrow. Cycle Continues.
body: The ratoon roots left in the ground push up new stalks. Compost from bagasse and press waste is spread back onto the fields. The same soil that fed this cup will feed the next - season after season, nothing wasted.
facts: Ratoon crop regrows naturally;Compost returns to the field;Same roots for 3–5 seasons
